one_line_takeaway: Inference-free spectral analysis of LoRA weight deltas can perfectly fingerprint which fine-tuning objective was applied within a given training method (e.g., DPO) and weakly-to-moderately predicts downstream harmful compliance, but a monitor trained on one manufacturing method (DPO) catastrophically fails -- with systematic sign inversion -- to generalize to a structurally different method (activation-steering injection), so any deployed weight-space monitor needs per-method calibration rather than a single classifier.
key_finding: Within a single training method (DPO), weight-space geometry perfectly encodes which objective was used (AUC 1.00 across all six pairwise comparisons) and training objective is the dominant axis of variation in weight-delta space (PC1, orthogonal to training duration on PC2) -- but this does not generalize across manufacturing methods: a DPO-trained classifier scores AUC 0.00 on steering-derived adapters, a systematic inversion (every steering adapter scored as more healthy than every DPO adapter), showing DPO and activation-steering injection perturb weight space in geometrically opposite directions. Geometry links to behavior only conditionally: for inverted-harmlessness DPO, weight-space drift probability correlates with HEx-PHI attack success rate at rho=0.72, but the same relationship does not hold for inverted-helpfulness adapters (rho=0.37) because HEx-PHI measures harmful compliance, not helpfulness erosion -- an evaluation-instrument mismatch rather than absence of a geometry-behavior link. The steering-derived adapters' behavioral evaluation was confounded: activation-steering injection caused generation collapse (incoherent output) at all tested intensities on Llama-3.2-3B, which Llama-Guard-3-1B misclassified as 'unsafe' (ASR 0.994) while GPT-4o correctly scored 0/300 as harmful, confirming the elevated ASR was a Guard artifact, not real harm.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not the cited Zhong & Raghunathan work it extends) is to show that low-rank spectral features of LoRA weight deltas can identify WHICH fine-tuning objective was applied (not just that drift occurred), and to test whether that geometric signal predicts downstream harmful behavior. On a pre-registered experiment with 38 manufactured LoRA adapters (Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct), a logistic regression classifier on per-layer spectral features (norms, stable rank, singular-value entropy, effective rank, singular-vector cosine alignment to a healthy centroid) achieves AUC 1.00 for binary drift detection and all six pairwise objective comparisons within the DPO training method, and near-perfect ordinal severity ranking (Spearman rho >= 0.956). PCA on flattened weight deltas shows training objective is PC1 (20.6% variance, AUC 1.00 for objective separation), orthogonal to training duration on PC2 (rho=0.589). The authors also report that direction/shape features (not magnitude) carry objective identity, that q_proj and v_proj specialize (query-projection detects that drift occurred, value-projection identifies which objective, e.g. AUC 0.50 vs 0.83 on the hardest within-method split), and that a DPO-trained classifier fails completely (AUC 0.00, a systematic inversion) when tested on activation-steering-derived adapters, demonstrating no cross-method generalization. In a behavioral evaluation phase, DPO-inverted-harmlessness adapters show elevated harmful compliance on HEx-PHI (mean ASR 0.266 vs healthy 0.112, delta +0.154) with near-perfect dose-response (rho=0.986), and weight-space drift probability correlates with HEx-PHI ASR at rho=0.72 (N=24 non-steered adapters, p<0.001).
threat_model: The threat model is fine-tuning-based alignment subversion: an adversary (or a careless practitioner) fine-tunes an aligned LLM via LoRA/DPO on a small number of examples with inverted safety or helpfulness preferences (following Qi et al.'s finding that as few as 10 adversarial examples can compromise safety guardrails), producing a model that behaviorally complies with harmful requests. The paper's proposed defense is inference-free weight-space monitoring: inspecting adapter weight deltas at upload/deployment time (before running the model on any prompts) to detect that fine-tuning occurred and to identify which training objective was used, as a cheaper/faster alternative or complement to expensive behavioral red-teaming. A second threat vector considered is activation-steering-derived LoRA adapters (SVD-injected steering vectors, no gradient descent) as an alternative attack/manufacturing method, used to test whether a monitor trained on DPO-manufactured drift generalizes to a structurally different attack.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitations: (1) single base model (Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct only; generalization to other architectures/sizes or full fine-tuning is untested); (2) small per-class test-set sizes (2-5 adapters) produce degenerate bootstrap CIs at perfect classification, meaning the current tasks may be too easy; (3) all drift is deliberately manufactured, not organic drift from distributional shift or data contamination; (4) behavioral scoring used Llama-Guard-3-1B instead of the pre-registered Guard-3-8B, requiring GPT-4o calibration to catch a false-positive failure mode; (5) HEx-PHI is the wrong evaluation instrument for inverted-helpfulness adapters, so the null behavioral result there may reflect instrument mismatch rather than absence of drift; (6) the pre-registered centroid-degradation hypothesis was not evaluated due to a pipeline/output error; (7) mixed-objective (superposed) adapters are untested, so linear decomposability of geometric signatures is unknown. Additionally, the cross-method AUC 0.00 result is confounded by the steering method itself producing broken/incoherent generation, so whether the geometric inversion is a general property of non-DPO training or an artifact of a broken injection method is explicitly left open by the authors.

datasets_used: HH-RLHF (Anthropic, harmlessness and helpfulness preference pairs)
HEx-PHI (330 harmful instructions across 11 categories, Qi et al.)
quantitative_results: Binary drift detection (healthy vs all-drifted): AUC 1.00, bootstrap 95% CI [1.00,1.00], 23 train/11 test adapters, zero misclassifications

All six pairwise objective-category comparisons: AUC 1.00 each (Healthy vs Inv.Harmlessness, Healthy vs Inv.Helpfulness, Healthy vs Refusal Steering, Inv.Harmlessness vs Inv.Helpfulness, Inv.Harmlessness vs Refusal Steering, Inv.Helpfulness vs Refusal Steering)
Ordinal severity ranking (Spearman rho vs step count): inverted harmlessness rho=0.976, inverted helpfulness rho=1.000, refusal steering rho=0.956 (all p<0.01)
Hardest within-method split (Inv.Harmlessness vs Inv.Helpfulness) by module: q_proj only AUC 0.50 (chance), v_proj only AUC 0.83, both combined AUC 1.00
Cross-method generalization: DPO-trained classifier on steering-derived adapters, AUC 0.00 (n_bootstrap=972, CI [0.00,0.00])
PCA on 18 DPO adapters: PC1 explains 20.6% variance, separates objective type AUC 1.00, rho=-0.056 (p=0.83) vs step count; PC2 tracks training magnitude rho=0.589 (p=0.01), objective-type AUC=0.55
Weight-space vs activation-space probe directional alignment: max cosine ~0.098 across all layers/adapters, mean alignment ratio 0.015
HEx-PHI ASR: DPO inverted harmlessness mean 0.266 vs healthy mean 0.112 (delta +0.154, pre-registered threshold +0.10); GPT-4o calibration confirms elevation +0.113
DPO inverted helpfulness: mean ASR 0.153, elevation +0.041 (below +0.10 threshold), dose-response rho=0.37 (p~0.47, not significant)
Dose-response for inverted harmlessness: Spearman rho(step, ASR)=0.9856 across six step levels (50-2000)

Geometry-behavior correlation: Phase-3 drift probability vs HEx-PHI ASR, rho=0.72 (N=24 non-steered adapters, p<0.001); including steered-refusal adapters inflates to rho=0.84 but authors flag this as a Guard artifact, not real
Frobenius norm vs ASR within inverted-harmlessness adapters: rho ~0.99 (exploratory)
Feature importance: direction features (SV cosine to healthy centroid) carry 10x higher mean logistic-regression coefficient than shape features and 30x higher than magnitude features
Preflight study (N=6): shape features AUC 1.00 for objective separation (leave-one-out); magnitude features AUC 0.275 (below chance); q_proj alone AUC 0.00 for objective separation
Steering-adapter behavioral artifact: Llama-Guard-3-1B ASR 0.994 on steered-refusal adapters vs GPT-4o calibrated ASR 0.000 (generation collapse, not real harmful compliance)
Proof-of-concept (Appendix A, N=7): spectral classifier AUC 1.00 (leave-one-out); a 15-scenario sycophancy behavioral test scored AUC 0.083 (below chance) on the same population
baselines_compared: Healthy SFT baseline adapters (n=10) fine-tuned on HH-RLHF chosen responses, used as the non-drifted reference class for all classification and ASR comparisons

Llama-Guard-3-1B as the primary harmful-compliance judge, cross-validated against a GPT-4o calibration pass (LLM-as-judge, per Zheng et al. MT-Bench methodology) which revealed a false-positive failure mode on steering-adapter incoherent output

Magnitude-only vs shape-only vs direction/all-feature classifier splits, and q_proj-only vs v_proj-only vs combined module splits, used as internal ablation baselines to localize where objective-identity signal lives
